sources_note: Growing guidance on this page draws on Citrus Australia, Australian state agriculture departments, university horticulture extension, and the Rare Fruit Council of Australia and WANATCA archives.

core.intro: Across Australia the basics of growing oranges are much the same: full sun, free draining soil, a grafted named variety, and steady feeding and watering. The state sections below cover the climate, the main growing regions, harvest timing and the pests where you are. The notes here apply anywhere in the country.

sections.heading: Choosing a variety
sections.body: Sweet oranges fall into four groups. Navels (Washington Navel, Cara Cara, Navelina, Newhall and Lanes Late) are the seedless, easy peel winter oranges and the best for fresh eating. Valencia and the other common oranges (Valencia, Valencia Late, Hamlin and Pineapple) ripen later and are the classic juicing oranges, with a high juice content that keeps its flavour. Blood oranges (Tarocco, Arnold and Maltese) carry a red, berry like flesh and juice, though the colour only develops where winter nights turn cold. Sour or bitter oranges (Seville, bergamot and chinotto) are too sharp to eat fresh and are grown for marmalade and cooking, so if you want a sharp citrus for the kitchen a lemon or lime may suit you better. Decide whether you want fruit for the table, for juice, or for the kitchen, then pick a named variety to match.

sections.heading: Pollination and seeds: one tree is enough
sections.body: Sweet oranges are self fertile, so a single tree sets a full crop on its own with no second tree or pollinator needed. Navels go further still: they are male sterile and set their fruit without any pollination, which is why a Washington Navel or a pink fleshed Cara Cara is usually seedless. The main reason a so called seedless orange picks up the odd pip is bees carrying pollen from other citrus nearby, so seediness rises where several different citrus grow close together. For a small garden that makes an orange one of the easiest fruit trees of all, because you never have to find room for a partner.

sections.heading: Why orange trees are grafted
sections.body: Almost every orange tree is grafted, with a named scion budded onto a separate rootstock, because seedling oranges are slow to bear, not true to type and prone to root troubles. The rootstock quietly sets much of how a tree performs: its eventual size, its cold and drought tolerance, how it copes with heavy, salty or disease ridden soil, and even the quality of the fruit. Common Australian rootstocks include trifoliata (Poncirus trifoliata, which tolerates cold and wet ground and suits lime free soils), citrange, Cleopatra mandarin, and, for a genuinely small tree, the dwarfing Flying Dragon. You rarely pick the rootstock yourself, but it is worth asking the nursery which one a tree is on and choosing a dwarf rootstock if it is bound for a pot.

sections.heading: Planting and soil
sections.body: Oranges want full sun and free draining soil with a slightly acidic pH of about 6 to 6.5. They are frost tender, especially in their first few years, so give a young tree the warmest, most sheltered spot you have. On heavy or wet ground, plant on a low mound so water never sits around the roots, and always set the tree at the depth it grew in the nursery rather than burying the trunk or the graft union. Mulch well to hold moisture and feed the soil, but keep the mulch a hand width clear of the trunk to avoid collar rot. Container grown trees can go in through most of the year as long as you keep the water up over summer (see our guide on when to plant).

sections.heading: Water and feeding
sections.body: Oranges are thirsty, hungry trees. Water matters most through flowering, fruit set and the long summer of fruit sizing, so water young and bearing trees deeply and regularly over the warm months and ease off in winter. Drainage counts as much as watering, because soggy soil invites the root and collar rot (Phytophthora) that kills citrus, so never let a tree stand in water. On feeding, citrus are heavy nitrogen users: a mature tree wants roughly half a kilo to a kilogram of actual nitrogen a year, built up gradually from about 100 grams in the early years, and split across several feeds rather than dumped at once. Feed in late winter just before the spring flush, again in spring, and a final time in early summer, and avoid feeding in late autumn, which pushes soft growth into the cold and can spoil the fruit. Use a complete citrus fertiliser plus compost or well rotted manure. Watch too for the yellow leaves of zinc, iron and manganese deficiency, which is common on sandy and alkaline soils where these trace elements lock up, and correct it with a citrus trace element or chelated spray.

sections.heading: Harvest, and storing on the tree
sections.body: Oranges do not ripen any further once they are picked, so leave a fruit on the tree until it actually tastes sweet rather than picking on colour. Their great advantage over soft fruit is that they store on the tree: a ripe orange hangs for weeks, and often months, slowly sweetening, so you can pick a few at a time as you need them rather than all at once. A grafted tree usually carries its first worthwhile crop within about three to four years of planting, with navels colouring through the cooler months and Valencia hanging on into the warm season.

sections.heading: Eating and juicing
sections.body: Match the orange to the job. Navels are the eating orange, sweet and seedless and easy to peel, but their juice turns bitter within hours of squeezing because of a compound called limonin, so eat them fresh and drink any navel juice straight away. Valencia and the common oranges are the juicers, with sweet juice that holds its flavour, which is what makes them the classic breakfast orange. Blood oranges bring a deeper, berry like flavour and a striking red juice, at their best where the nights turn cold while the fruit matures.

sections.heading: Buying tips
sections.body: Buy a named, grafted tree rather than an unnamed seedling. Seedling oranges take many years to fruit and may not come true, while a grafted named variety on a suitable rootstock fruits within a few years and to type. Check the label for the variety and, if you can, the rootstock, and pick a dwarfing rootstock if the tree is destined for a pot or a small courtyard. The in stock table above shows the named varieties for sale right now and what they cost.

faqs.q: Can I grow an orange tree in a pot?
faqs.a: Yes, as long as you start with a tree on a dwarfing rootstock and give it a large container (at least 40 cm across), a free draining mix and at least six hours of sun. Pots dry out and leach nutrients fast, so water whenever the top of the mix is dry and feed a little every month through the warmer half of the year. Refresh the top of the mix or pot up every couple of years.

faqs.q: How big does an orange tree get, and can I keep it small?
faqs.a: A standard orange can reach 4 to 6 metres, but it takes pruning well and is easily held to head height for picking and netting, while a tree grown for a pot stays much smaller. Prune lightly after harvest to shape the tree and open up its centre, which also lets in the light and air that keep fruit clean.

faqs.q: Why does my orange tree drop lots of small fruit in early summer?
faqs.a: This is normal. After flowering an orange sets far more tiny fruit than it could ever ripen, then sheds the excess in early summer, keeping only the load it can carry. A heavy natural drop is usually nothing to worry about, but it gets worse if the tree runs short of water or food while the fruit is small, so keep the watering and feeding steady from flowering through early summer.

faqs.q: Can an orange still be ripe when the skin is green?
faqs.a: Yes. Orange skin only turns fully orange when nights are cool, so in warm districts a perfectly ripe orange can keep a green tinge, and a ripe fruit left on the tree can even green up again in spring (a harmless process called regreening). Colour is a poor guide to ripeness, so taste a fruit before deciding the tree is ready.

WA.intro: Growing oranges in Western Australia. The Mediterranean south west, with its hot dry summers and mild wet winters, is close to ideal for citrus, and an orange tree is a fixture of the backyard. Oranges are grown commercially from the hills around Perth north to the Gascoyne.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: The south west of WA has the warm dry summers and mild winters that citrus loves, and oranges crop reliably across the region, in the ground or in a pot. Further north, the warm subtropical Gascoyne around Carnarvon ripens fruit early, ahead of the rest of the state, and even the tropical far north at Kununurra can grow citrus. The hardest part of WA citrus is usually the soil rather than the climate, because much of the coastal plain is deep, hungry sand.

sections.heading: Where oranges grow in WA
sections.body: The traditional citrus districts run north from the Perth hills and the Swan Valley through Bindoon and the Chittering Valley to Gingin, with newer orchards around Moora and Dandaragan, and a long established fruit district at Harvey to the south. Far to the north, Carnarvon on the Gascoyne River grows early citrus for the southern markets.

sections.heading: Harvest window in WA
sections.body: WA oranges are picked through much of the year but peak in the colder winter months. The warm Gascoyne around Carnarvon crops earliest, from about May, while the cooler south west runs later in the year, so between the districts the state has fresh oranges over a long season.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in WA
sections.body: Mediterranean fruit fly, not the Queensland fruit fly of the eastern states, is WA's headline citrus pest. It is endemic right across the state and uses citrus as its main overwintering host, and in several shires its control is required by law. Citrus gall wasp is a newer arrival: it has been in backyards around Perth since 2013 but, importantly, has not reached WA's commercial orchards or most country districts, which is why regional growers are asked to keep a lookout for it. WA also stays free of citrus canker and citrus black spot, kept out by the state's quarantine.

sections.heading: Buying and shipping to WA
sections.body: Western Australia keeps tight border quarantine on live plants, and citrus is a known carrier of pests and diseases the state wants to keep out, so many eastern nurseries will not post citrus here at all. The in stock table above is filtered to the sellers that can legally ship oranges to WA.

faqs.q: How do I keep fruit fly out of my backyard oranges in WA?
faqs.a: Because Mediterranean fruit fly is established statewide and treats citrus as a favoured target, a backyard tree needs active management: hang baits or traps from spring, pick the fruit as it ripens rather than letting it hang and soften, and promptly bin or solarise any fallen or stung fruit so the next generation has nowhere to breed. In some shires you are legally obliged to control it.

faqs.q: Can oranges cope with WA's sandy soils?
faqs.a: Yes, oranges grow well in free draining sand, better than many fruit trees, but the sand holds little goodness, so dig in plenty of compost before planting, mulch every year, and feed little and often through the warmer months. A soil wetting agent helps if the sand has turned water repellent, and a citrus trace element spray fixes the yellow leaves that sandy, alkaline soils often bring on.

QLD.intro: Growing oranges in Queensland. Queensland is a major citrus state, but its real strength is mandarins and limes; for oranges the warm, drier inland gives the best fruit, while the humid coast and tropics are harder going.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Oranges do best in Queensland's warm but drier inland, where the Central Burnett has long been one of Australia's important citrus districts. On the humid coast and through the lowland tropics, higher disease pressure and softer, more blemished rind make a clean orange harder to grow, and those areas tend to favour mandarins and limes. A navel or a Valencia is the most reliable orange across the state.

sections.heading: Where oranges grow in QLD
sections.body: The heart of the Queensland citrus industry is the Central Burnett around Gayndah and Mundubbera, inland from Bundaberg, with other plantings near Emerald in the Central Highlands and, in the far north, Mareeba, where limes lead. Gayndah is one of the oldest citrus towns in the country.

sections.heading: Harvest window in QLD
sections.body: Queensland's warmth brings the orange season on early. Navels in the warmer districts can be ready from about April or May, ahead of the southern states, while Valencia and the common oranges follow and hang on into the warmer months.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in QLD
sections.body: Queensland fruit fly is endemic here and will use oranges as a host, so backyard fruit needs protection. Citrus gall wasp is a Queensland native, long a pest of the state's citrus, and the wet season brings more fungal rind blemish in the humid coastal districts than in the dry inland. Queensland also carries the country's most serious citrus biosecurity story: citrus canker was found at Emerald in 2004 and eradicated after hundreds of thousands of trees were destroyed, and a later northern outbreak was wiped out so that Australia was declared free of the disease in 2021.

faqs.q: Why will my blood oranges not turn red in Queensland?
faqs.a: Blood oranges develop their red flesh only when nights are cold while the fruit is ripening, and most of Queensland stays too warm for that, so the flesh comes out pale or only lightly streaked. The tree is healthy and the fruit is good to eat, it simply will not colour up the way it would further south. If you want dependable colour, grow a navel or a Valencia for flavour and juice and treat a blood orange as a bonus in the coldest snaps.

faqs.q: Do I need to be careful moving citrus plants or fruit in Queensland?
faqs.a: Yes. After the canker outbreaks, and with fruit fly about, Queensland asks people not to carry citrus plants, cuttings or even fruit interstate without checking the rules first, and to report any unusual leaf or fruit lesions to Biosecurity Queensland. Buying from a registered nursery and leaving budwood to the professionals keeps your own patch and the wider industry safe.

NSW.intro: Growing oranges in New South Wales. NSW is the heart of the Australian orange, with the great inland irrigation districts growing more oranges than anywhere else in the country.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Oranges thrive in the hot, dry, irrigated inland of NSW, where long warm summers ripen sweet, well coloured fruit and the dry air keeps disease down. They grow on the humid coast and around Sydney too, though there the fruit carries more skin blemish, and in the cold tablelands a young tree needs protection from frost. Irrigation is essential inland, so the main districts sit along the rivers.

sections.heading: Where oranges grow in NSW
sections.body: The Riverina, in the Murrumbidgee Irrigation Area around Griffith and Leeton, is the single largest citrus district in Australia and the country's biggest orange producing region, growing navels and Valencias on a grand scale. Other plantings run along the Murray, through central NSW around Narromine, and on the coast.

sections.heading: Harvest window in NSW
sections.body: The NSW navel season runs through winter, from about May to October, while Valencia is the late orange, maturing from spring and held on the tree to be picked right through summer. That long split season is why the Riverina can supply oranges for much of the year.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in NSW
sections.body: Citrus gall wasp, a native insect once limited to Queensland and northern NSW, is now established right across the state, from the coast and the Sydney basin to the inland orange districts, and is the backyard pest most growers meet first. Queensland fruit fly is also present and will attack citrus, and rind blemishing pests such as Kelly's citrus thrips can mark the fruit in some districts. Citrus canker, the disease that struck Queensland, is exotic to NSW and not present, though it is the reason for the rules on moving citrus.

faqs.q: How do I deal with citrus gall wasp on a backyard orange tree?
faqs.a: Cut out the woody galls on the stems before the new wasps fly out, ideally in late autumn and early winter (the prune in June rule), and bin or solarise the prunings so the grubs inside cannot emerge. Go easy on high nitrogen feeding in spring, because the soft new growth it pushes is exactly what the wasp lays its eggs into, and feed in late autumn and winter instead.

faqs.q: Which oranges suit the hot inland versus the cooler parts of NSW?
faqs.a: In the hot inland almost any orange does well, and the heat finishes Valencia and the juicing oranges beautifully. Blood oranges such as Tarocco and Arnold need cold nights to colour, so they reward the cooler southern and elevated districts more than the milder, humid coast. On the frost prone tablelands, start with a hardy navel and shelter young trees through their first couple of winters.

VIC.intro: Growing oranges in Victoria. Victoria is a major navel state, with the warm, dry Sunraysia along the Murray at its centre, and its cool nights make it one of the best places in the country to grow a blood orange.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Victoria's warm, dry north west around Sunraysia has the heat and the sunshine that ripen excellent navels under irrigation. Cooler central and southern Victoria, including Melbourne, ripens fruit more slowly and can frost a young tree, so site a new orange in the warmest, most sheltered corner you have. Valencia, the latest orange of all to ripen, can struggle to finish in the coldest southern districts.

sections.heading: Where oranges grow in VIC
sections.body: The Murray Valley around Mildura, Robinvale and Red Cliffs is Victoria's citrus heartland and one of Australia's major navel districts, growing the bulk of the state's oranges. Cooler districts such as the Goulburn Valley grow a little citrus too, but in small numbers beside the warm north west.

sections.heading: Harvest window in VIC
sections.body: Victoria ripens later than the warm northern states. Navels colour and sweeten through winter and into spring, and Valencia, the last to ripen, is often left hanging well into the warm months before it is picked. The long, cool autumn and winter is exactly what gives Victorian fruit its quality.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in VIC
sections.body: Citrus gall wasp has spread south in recent years and is now found through Victoria's citrus districts and common on suburban Melbourne trees, so prune the galls out before the wasps emerge in spring. Queensland fruit fly has also become established in the Sunraysia district, where the long standing pest free area was wound back in 2024, so backyard fruit now needs fly protection. Young trees in the cooler south are vulnerable to frost and worth protecting through their first winters.

faqs.q: Do oranges fruit reliably around Melbourne?
faqs.a: Yes, navels in particular do well. The Washington Navel has a low heat requirement and ripens early, and planting an early Navelina, a mid season Washington and a late Lanes Late spreads the navel harvest across much of the year. Valencia is the one to be patient with, because it ripens last and needs to hang a long time in the cool before it sweetens, so leave it on the tree.

faqs.q: Why do blood oranges colour so well in Victoria?
faqs.a: Blood oranges only develop their red pigment when the fruit ripens through cold nights, and the sharp gap between warm days and near freezing nights in inland and southern Victoria is just what they need. The same variety grown in a warm, humid climate stays pale inside, which is why a Tarocco or an Arnold ripened through a cool Victorian winter can be so deeply coloured.
